1: A direct revelation: “You are old and advanced in years, and there remains yet very much land to possess.” YHWH’s word to the aging Joshua that the conquest is incomplete is the central theological tension that Hebrews unpacks. Hebrews 4:8: “For if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on” (citing Psalms 95:11). The “much land that remains” is not a failure of the conquest but an embedded pointer: the Canaan-rest is real but provisional, a sign of a greater rest still to come. Christ is the Joshua who will give what the original Joshua could not — the final, unending, perfect rest of God’s dwelling with his people (Revelation 21:3-4).
2: A type: the land that remains — the territory of the Philistines and the Geshurites. The Philistines represent the perennial enemy that Israel’s judges and kings will combat for the entire period of the monarchy — the threat that cannot be resolved by the initial conquest. David will definitively defeat the Philistines (2 Samuel 5:17-25; Joshua 8:1), but only after Israel has suffered centuries of Philistine oppression. The progressive conquest of remaining enemies across Israel’s history is the pattern of the Christian life: the decisive victory is won at the cross, but experiential possession of that victory is a progressive, lifelong reality (Philippians 3:12-14: “not that I have already obtained this or am already perfect, but I press on”).
3: A type: the Canaanite territory from the Shihor to Ekron and the five lords of the Philistines (Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, Ekron). The five Philistine city-states — the pentapolis — will become the symbolic enemy of Israel’s full covenantal possession from Judges through 1 Samuel. Goliath from Gath is the Philistine champion who will one day be defeated by the shepherd-king David (1 Sam 17). The fact that these domains are “considered Canaanite” places the Philistine presence within the larger framework of the as-yet-unconquered territory — the remaining work that points to the greater Joshua who will complete it.
4: A type: the land of the Canaanites and Mearah belonging to the Sidonians, to Aphek, to the boundary of the Amorites. Sidon is the great Phoenician city-state that will recur throughout Israel’s history, and whose territory Jesus will visit (Mark 7:24-31: the Syrophoenician woman at Tyre and Sidon). Jesus’s deliberate journeys into Gentile territories — Tyre, Sidon, the Decapolis — are the NT counterpart of the remaining-territory vision: the domains that the Canaanite conquest did not reach, Christ reaches personally and extends the covenant blessing.
5: A type: Gebel (Byblos) and all Lebanon to the east, to Lebo-hamath. Lebanon’s cedar forests were used by Solomon for the temple (1 Kings 5:6-10) — the remaining northern territory beyond Israel’s direct conquest becomes the source of materials for YHWH’s dwelling place. The territory that could not be conquered militarily is incorporated by trade and covenant into the temple project. The eschatological gathering of the nations’ wealth into the new Jerusalem (Isaiah 60:13: “the glory of Lebanon shall come to you”; Revelation 21:24: “the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it”) is the ultimate incorporation of the remaining territories.
6: A direct revelation: “I myself will drive them out from before the people of Israel; only allot the land to Israel for an inheritance as I have commanded you.” YHWH’s promise to complete the driving-out himself — even the remaining territories — is the basis for the inheritance distribution that follows. Philippians 1:6: “I am sure of this, that he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ.” The divine first-person commitment to complete what was begun is the ground of the inheritance-distribution: Joshua can allot the land as if the remaining enemies are already expelled because YHWH has pledged to do it. The believer can live in the full inheritance of Christ because the one who began the work pledges to complete it.
7: A type: “Now therefore divide this land for an inheritance to the nine tribes and the half-tribe of Manasseh.” The inheritance distribution — land allocated as gift, not earned by settlement — is the enacted form of the covenant promise. Ephesians 1:11: “In him we have obtained an inheritance, having been predestined according to the purpose of him who works all things according to the counsel of his will.” The land-lot process (the goral) that determines each tribe’s portion mirrors the election concept: the inheritance is determined by divine assignment, not human achievement or preference.
8: A type: the Reubenites and Gadites received their inheritance across the Jordan with Moses, as YHWH’s servant had given it to them. The Transjordanian tribes received their inheritance before crossing — they were given the promise and the land before entering Canaan proper. This anticipates the realized-eschatology dimension of the NT: believers are “blessed with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places” (Ephesians 1:3) now, before the final inheritance is physically possessed. The possession is complete in the divine declaration before it is experientially inhabited.
9: A type: the territory from Aroer on the Arnon to Medeba includes the plateau of Moab. The Transjordanian territory is defined precisely — from the boundary of Moab, through the plateau cities, to the border of the Ammonites. This precision in describing territory that Israel will hold signifies that every inch of the covenant land is accounted for by the divine land-lord. Hebrews 11:16 speaks of the patriarchs as desiring “a better country, that is, a heavenly one,” and the precise enumeration of earthly territory points to the specificity of the heavenly inheritance — God’s gifts are not vague but precisely given to precisely named recipients.
10: A type: all the cities of Sihon king of the Amorites as far as the boundary of the Ammonites. The towns of the former Sihon-kingdom are now Reuben’s and Gad’s inheritance — the anti-kingdom’s domain becomes the covenant people’s home. 2 Corinthians 10:4-5: “The weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh but have divine power to destroy strongholds... take every thought captive to obey Christ.” The strongholds of Sihon become Israel’s towns — the strongholds of the enemy become the territory of Christ’s people.
11: A type: Gilead and the territory of the Geshurites and Maacathites and all Mount Hermon and all Bashan to Salecah. The Bashan territory — Og’s former kingdom — now belongs to the half-tribe of Manasseh. Mount Hermon, the highest point in the region, was the location of Jesus’s transfiguration (Matthew 17:1-8; the Hermon-transfiguration identification is widely accepted). The mountain of the defeated giant-king Og became the mountain of the glorious divine Son — the geography of conquest transformed into the geography of revelation.
12: A type: all the kingdom of Og in Bashan — the remnant of the Rephaim, whom Moses defeated. The repeated mention of Og as the last of the Rephaim (cf. Joshua 12:4; Deuteronomy 3:11) establishes that the conquest of Canaan eliminated the line of the nephilim/Rephaim giants. In the NT, the Rephaim/nephilim tradition (Genesis 6:1-4; Numbers 13:33) underlies the language of spiritual powers: the “giants” of the spiritual realm are the rulers and authorities Paul describes in Ephesians 6:12. Christ’s defeat of the last Rephaim’s descendants — the cosmic powers aligned against humanity — is the fulfillment of the defeat-of-Og type.
13: A type: the Israelites did not drive out the Geshurites or the Maacathites; Geshur and Maacath live in the midst of Israel to this day. This is the paradigmatic failed-conquest notice: despite the allotment, the actual expulsion was incomplete. The Geshurites will appear again when Absalom flees to Geshur (his mother’s homeland, 2 Samuel 3:3) after killing Amnon — the unconquered territory becomes the refuge of David’s most dangerous son. The remaining Canaanites who corrupt Israel’s covenant life are the type of the “sin that so easily entangles” (Hebrews 12:1) that believers must actively resist even in the realized inheritance.
14: A direct revelation: “To the tribe of Levi alone Moses gave no inheritance; the offerings by fire to YHWH, the God of Israel, are their inheritance, as he said to them.” The Levitical inheritance is YHWH himself — his offerings, his service, his presence. This principle has two NT fulfillments: (1) Christ as our inheritance — “YHWH is my portion” (Lamentations 3:24; Psalms 73:26) becomes the believer’s reality in Christ, who gives himself as the inheritance of all his people; (2) the church as a royal priesthood (1 Peter 2:9) has no earthly territorial inheritance but is given God himself. Revelation 21:3: “God himself will be with them as their God” — the Levitical pattern of divine-presence-as-inheritance is universalized to all God’s people in the new creation.
15: A type: the allotment to the tribe of Reuben — the firstborn who forfeited the double inheritance (Genesis 49:3-4: “Reuben... unstable as water, you shall not have preeminence”). Reuben receives a portion — not disinherited, but not the preeminent inheritance he might have had. Jacob’s prophetic word at his deathbed (Genesis 49:3-4) is here enacted: the firstborn receives a territory, but Judah will hold the preeminence (Genesis 49:10: “the scepter shall not depart from Judah”). The pattern of the firstborn giving way to the chosen son is the recurring pattern that reaches its fulfillment in Christ, the elect one through whom all the tribes receive their inheritance.
16: A type: the specific cities of Reuben’s allotment — Aroer, Dibon, Bamoth-baal, Beth-baal-meon. The inclusion of “Baal” in city names (Beth-baal-meon = “house of the lord of the dwelling”) in Reuben’s territory signals the ongoing Baal-worship pressure that will corrupt the Transjordanian tribes in the Judges period (and Reuben will largely disappear from Israel’s tribal life). The danger of inheriting cities named after Baal is the danger of habitation shaping worship — the pull of the place’s identity on its inhabitants. Christ’s overcoming of Baal-worship (Elijah’s Carmel victory, 1 Kings 18) is the prophetic preview of the complete displacement of every competing name by the name of Jesus (Philippians 2:10).
17: A type: Heshbon and its cities on the plateau — Dibon, Bamoth-baal, Beth-baal-meon. Heshbon was Sihon’s capital, the center of Amorite power east of the Jordan. The fact that former Sihon-cities become Reubenite inheritance demonstrates the complete transfer of sovereignty: the anti-kingdom’s administrative center becomes the covenant people’s home. The NT pattern is 2 Corinthians 10:5: “we destroy arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God, and take every thought captive to obey Christ” — the strongholds of the enemy become the domain of Christ’s obedience.
18: A type: Jahaz, Kedemoth, Mephaath — additional Reubenite cities. These were the sites of Moses’s abortive peace-mission to Sihon (Deuteronomy 2:26-29: Moses sent messengers from “the wilderness of Kedemoth” asking permission to pass peacefully). Sihon refused, fought, and was defeated. The city from which the peace-offer was sent becomes part of the inheritance of those who prevailed. The principle: when God’s offer of peace is rejected, judgment falls; and the very ground from which the offer was made becomes the inheritance of those who were faithful. Christ’s offer of peace to Jerusalem (Matthew 23:37-39) follows the same pattern — rejection followed by judgment, and the inheritance passing to those who receive him.
19: A type: Kiriathaim, Sibmah, and Zereth-shahar on the hill of the valley. Sibmah will appear in the prophets as a vineyard associated with mourning and judgment (Isaiah 16:8-9; Jeremiah 48:32: “I will weep for you as Jazer weeps, O vine of Sibmah”) when Moab devastates it. The territory given to Reuben as inheritance will eventually pass through Moabite destruction — the Reubenite failure to hold the inheritance is the type of the apostate who does not maintain possession of their allotted inheritance. Hebrews 3-4 uses the wilderness generation’s failure to enter Canaan-rest as the paradigm warning against failing to maintain covenant faithfulness.
20: A type: Beth-peor, the slopes of Pisgah, Beth-jeshimoth — Reubenite cities. Beth-peor is the most fraught site on this list: it is where Israel committed sexual immorality with Moabite women and worshiped Baal of Peor (Numbers 25:1-9), resulting in a plague that killed 24,000. Moses is buried “in the valley opposite Beth-peor” (Deuteronomy 34:6). Reuben’s inheritance includes the site of Israel’s most catastrophic sexual-idolatry sin — a perpetual warning within the geography of the allotment. Christ’s intercessory high priesthood (Hebrews 7:25) is the antitype of Phinehas’s atonement act at Peor (Numbers 25:7-13): the priest who “always lives to make intercession” permanently resolves what Peor only temporarily checked.
21: A type: all the cities of the plateau and the whole kingdom of Sihon, including the five princes of Midian — Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba. The five Midianite princes killed alongside Sihon at this campaign are also killed in the later Midianite war of Numbers 31:8. Balaam son of Beor is explicitly noted as killed (Joshua 13:22). The defeat of the Midianite princes who aligned with Sihon connects the Transjordanian conquest to the Balaam episode of Numbers 22-24: the entire network of opposition to Israel — Moabite king, Midianite princes, professional diviner — is brought under the same defeat. Christ’s triumph encompasses every form of spiritual opposition: political, cultic, and occultic.
22: A type: Balaam son of Beor, the diviner, was killed with the sword by the people of Israel among the rest of their slain. Balaam, whose mouth YHWH controlled to bless Israel instead of cursing (Num 22-24), nonetheless counseled Moab to corrupt Israel through Baal-Peor (Numbers 31:16; Revelation 2:14: “you have some there who hold the teaching of Balaam”). The one who could not curse Israel directly corrupted them through sexual-idolatry bait — and is ultimately killed in the Midianite war. 2 Peter 2:15-16 names “the way of Balaam” as the path of the false prophet who loves gain from wrongdoing. The death of Balaam is the type of the definitive end of the false-prophet spirit, fully overcome in Christ who is the faithful True Witness (Revelation 3:14).
23: A type: the Jordan boundary of the Reubenite inheritance, their cities and villages. The Jordan as boundary is also a baptismal type throughout Joshua — the river that must be crossed to enter the inheritance. Reuben’s territory is on the far side of the Jordan from the main inheritance of Israel; they have an inheritance, but separated by the river from the central sanctuary’s access. This spatial separation will eventually contribute to the altar-controversy of Joshua 22, where the Transjordanian tribes build a witness-altar because they fear future generations will be denied access to YHWH’s covenant. The anxiety about separation from the covenant presence is answered in Christ: Romans 8:38-39 declares that nothing can separate believers from the love of God in Christ Jesus.
24: A type: the allotment to the tribe of Gad — the tribe whose name means “fortune/luck” (Genesis 30:11, Leah: “a troop comes!”). Gad’s fighters are described in 1 Chronicles 12:8 as “mighty and experienced warriors, expert with shield and spear, whose faces were like the faces of lions, and who were swift as gazelles on the mountains” — they join David at Ziklag. The warrior character of Gad fits the Transjordanian territory that requires ongoing vigilance against Ammonite and Moabite pressure. Christ as the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5) is the ultimate warrior-king to whom all tribal warrior traditions point.
25: A type: Gad’s territory included Jazer, all the cities of Gilead, half the land of the Ammonites to Aroer near Rabbah. Rabbah is the Ammonite capital — the later site of Uriah the Hittite’s death engineered by David (2 Samuel 11:14-17), and the city that David will besiege and capture (2 Samuel 12:26-31). David’s Rabbah campaign is one of the ethical low points of his reign and one of the narrative high points of the cost of sin in Israel’s covenant life. The territory around Rabbah, allotted to Gad, becomes the backdrop for the David-Bathsheba-Uriah tragedy — the conquest territory that should have been peacefully settled becomes the site of covenant violation.
26: A type: from Heshbon to Ramath-mizpah and Betonim, from Mahanaim to the boundary of Debir. Mahanaim is where Jacob encountered “the camp of God” (machane Elohim) and named the place Mahanaim (“two camps,” Genesis 32:2). It becomes the capital of Ish-bosheth’s rival kingdom (2 Samuel 2:8) and David’s refuge during Absalom’s rebellion (2 Samuel 17:24). The site of Jacob’s angelic encounter, located in Gad’s allotment, carries the patriarchal promise through the covenant history: from Jacob’s night wrestling to David’s exile to the final angelic camp around God’s people (Revelation 7:9-12).
27: A type: in the valley — Beth-haram, Beth-nimrah, Succoth, Zaphon — the rest of the kingdom of Sihon, with the Jordan as its eastern boundary to the tip of the Sea of Kinnereth. Succoth is Jacob’s first stop after his reconciliation with Esau (Genesis 33:17: “Jacob journeyed to Succoth, built himself a house, and made booths for his livestock; therefore the place is called Succoth”). The patriarchal waystation becomes part of Gad’s territorial inheritance. The Feast of Booths (Sukkot) — the feast of temporary dwellings that commemorates the wilderness period — finds its NT fulfillment in John 7:37-39 (Jesus’s declaration on the last day of Sukkot: “if anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink”) and Revelation 21:3 (God’s tabernacle is with men).
28: A type: the inheritance of Gad, their cities and villages. The formulaic close of each tribal allotment — “their cities and villages” — signals the completed gift-transfer. The inheritance is both the named land and the settled life within it: cities (political-administrative reality) and villages (agricultural-communal reality). The NT inheritance is similarly both cosmic (“heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ,” Romans 8:17) and concrete (“the meek shall inherit the earth,” Matthew 5:5; “the new Jerusalem,” Rev 21-22). The OT formula of inheritance-completion anticipates the NT promise’s specificity and completeness.
29: A type: the half-tribe of Manasseh’s Transjordanian allotment. Manasseh is Joseph’s firstborn son whose birth and blessing were marked by extraordinary divine reversal (Genesis 48:13-20: Jacob deliberately crossed his hands to give Ephraim the greater blessing over Manasseh). The half-tribe across the Jordan becomes part of the Israelite identity — separated from the main tribal territory yet still fully within the covenant inheritance. The pattern of divided inheritance (half in Transjordan, half in Canaan) mirrors the “already/not yet” structure of the believer’s inheritance: possessing in part while awaiting full possession.
30: A type: the Manassite territory included Mahanaim, all Bashan — the entire kingdom of Og — and the sixty cities of Jair in Bashan. The “tent-villages of Jair” (chavvot Ya'ir) are the sixty Beshanian cities claimed by the Manassite hero Jair (Numbers 32:41; Deuteronomy 3:14). The number sixty is the same as the sixty queens and eighty concubines of Song of Solomon 6:8 — a number denoting abundance. The sixty-city allotment of Manasseh’s half-tribe in Og’s former domain is the largest single concentrated inheritance in the Transjordanian division, signaling that Manasseh’s share in the defeated giant-king’s territory is comprehensive.
31: A type: half of Gilead, Ashtaroth, and Edrei — the royal cities of Og in Bashan — for half the sons of Machir son of Manasseh. Machir is the firstborn of Manasseh (Numbers 26:29) and the most specifically military of the Manassite clans — “a man of war” (Numbers 32:39-41). The inheritance of Og’s royal cities — the capital of the giant-king — going specifically to the warrior-clan of Machir enacts the principle that the most formidable inheritances require the most committed faithfulness. In the NT, the “well done, good and faithful servant” (Matthew 25:21) leads to authority over “many things” — the inheritance proportioned to faithfulness in warfare against the enemy.
32: A type: these are the territories Moses distributed in the plains of Moab, across the Jordan east of Jericho. The formulaic close of the Transjordanian distribution connects it to Moses’s Mosaic authority — these inheritances have the same divine legitimacy as the western ones. The plains of Moab (in Moabite territory by definition, yet now the distribution-ground of Israel’s inheritance) signal that the covenant’s claim extends beyond Israel’s conventional boundaries: God distributes inheritance from unexpected ground. Christ’s mission extends beyond expected religious boundaries in the same pattern — distributing covenant inheritance from Gentile soil.
33: A direct revelation: “But to the tribe of Levi Moses gave no inheritance; YHWH, the God of Israel, is their inheritance, as he said to them.” The chapter closes with the Levitical formula of divine-presence-as-inheritance. This is the theological peak of chapter 13: in a chapter entirely about land distribution, the closing word is that one tribe receives not land but God himself. First Peter Joshua 2:9: “But you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession.” The NT church, as a royal priesthood, takes on the Levitical pattern: their inheritance is not earthly territory but the divine presence itself. Revelation 21:22: “I saw no temple in the city, for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb” — the Levitical temple-as-inheritance is fulfilled in the Lamb as the temple.
